rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical amidoselenation of alkenes (e.g., cyclohexene) with diphenyl diselenide and acetonitrile to give trans-1,2-selenyl-amido (acetamide) products via a seleniranium/Ritter-type cation pathway
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of PhSeSePh (or PhSe•) generates an electrophilic PhSe+ species that adds to the alkene to form a seleniranium ion; acetonitrile then performs Ritter-type nucleophilic capture

key_intermediates.name: 1IM2: cationic seleniranium ion (singlet, after SET oxidation)
IM3: nitrilium ion from MeCN attack on seleniranium
IM6: allylbenzene–PhSe+ seleniranium (branching point for Markovnikov vs anti-Markovnikov)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes PhSeSePh to PhSe• / PhSe+; PhSe+ adds to the alkene forming a seleniranium cation (IM1 → 1IM2), which is opened by acetonitrile to give a nitrilium ion (IM3); water addition and tautomerization (via TS4/TS5) deliver the trans-β-selenyl acetamide product.
rate_determining_step: Structural transition IM1 → 1IM2 (formation of the cationic seleniranium via single-electron oxidation), as supported by ADCH charge analysis and DFT

probe_reagents.name: BHT
probe_reagents.observation: Yield of 3n dropped from 92% to 55% (0.2 mmol) and 20% (0.4 mmol), indicating involvement of radical intermediates

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction suppressed to trace yield, consistent with a radical (PhSe•) intermediate

notes: DFT (PBE0(D3BJ)/Def2SVP, SMD/MeCN) maps both singlet (1IM2) and triplet (3IM2) seleniranium pathways; singlet cationic pathway is favored. For allylbenzene, anti-Markovnikov TS6 (16.45 kcal/mol) is slightly higher than Markovnikov TS7 (15.95 kcal/mol), and IM7 (–6.45) is more stable than IM8 (–4.63), rationalizing regioselectivity. ADCH atomic charge analysis on IM1→1IM2 for cyclopentene/cyclohexene/cycloheptene explains substrate-dependent yields: cyclohexene's negative C2 charge (–0.1664) best enables SET oxidation to the seleniranium cation, while cyclopentene's positive C2 charge blocks the conversion.
